theme: Signal Over Noise — Every Pixel Should Earn Its Place by Telling You Something True

ideas.title: Stat Card Trend Arrows — Tell Me If It's Getting Better or Worse
ideas.description: The stats bar shows Active/Idle/Error counts in isolation. A number without direction is just a fact. A number with a delta is a signal. Add ↑/↓ trend indicators comparing each count to the values at the previous sync. In StatsBar, add a prevCountsRef = useRef({}) that captures { active, idle, error } before the workers prop updates. On each render, compute deltas: const activeDelta = active - (prevCountsRef.current.active ?? active). Render below each count value: {delta !== 0 && 0 ? 'up' : 'down'}`}>{delta > 0 ? '↑' : '↓'}{Math.abs(delta)}}. Then update prevCountsRef.current after render via useEffect. CSS: .stat-delta { display:block; font-size:0.6rem; font-weight:700; line-height:1; margin-top:3px; } .stat-delta--up { color:#4ade80; } .stat-delta--down { color:#f87171; } .stat-delta { animation:delta-pop 0.4s cubic-bezier(0.34,1.56,0.64,1) both; } @keyframes delta-pop { from{opacity:0;transform:translateY(-5px)} to{opacity:1;transform:none} }. WHY: The stats bar currently answers 'how many?' A delta arrow answers 'is this trending toward incident or recovery?' That's the difference between a status page and a monitoring tool. Two characters — ↑2 — that make a manager's stomach drop or relax at a glance.

ideas.title: Task Duration Color Tiers — The Stuck Worker Is Glowing Red
ideas.description: Every worker card shows 'Working for 4m' in the same muted color whether it's been 4 minutes or 4 hours. A worker stuck on a single task for 3 hours is a blocker — visually identical to a worker who just clocked in. Fix this with duration-based color tiers on the worker-duration element. In WorkerCard, compute const durationMs = Date.now() - new Date(worker.updated_at).getTime() and const tier = durationMs < 30*60_000 ? 'normal' : durationMs < 2*3600_000 ? 'warn' : 'stuck'. Apply data-tier={tier} to the .worker-duration div. CSS: .worker-duration[data-tier='warn'] { color:#fbbf24; } .worker-duration[data-tier='stuck'] { color:#f87171; animation:duration-pulse 2.2s ease-in-out infinite; } @keyframes duration-pulse{0%,100%{opacity:1}50%{opacity:0.45}}. Add a small ⚠️ prefix when tier === 'stuck': {tier === 'stuck' && '⚠️ '}⏱️ {formatDuration(worker.updated_at)}. WHY: The dashboard's job is to surface blocked work before someone has to ask in Slack. A red-pulsing '⚠️ Working for 3h 22m' costs zero extra API calls and turns the card into an actual incident signal. Without this, every long task looks like progress.

ideas.title: Sync Ripple on Office Floor — The Data Just Updated
ideas.description: When a sync completes, the office floor changes silently. Workers teleport to new positions, statuses flicker — but there's no perceptual anchor telling you 'that just refreshed.' Add a radial pulse ripple from the center of the office floor outward, triggered once per sync completion. In App.jsx add const [justSynced, setJustSynced] = useState(false). At the end of syncFromGitHub (after setIsSyncing(false)), call setJustSynced(true) then setTimeout(() => setJustSynced(false), 1000). Pass as prop to Office. In Office.jsx render a <div className={'office-sync-ripple' + (justSynced ? ' office-sync-ripple--active' : '')} aria-hidden='true' /> inside .office-floor. CSS: .office-sync-ripple { position:absolute; left:50%; top:50%; width:0; height:0; pointer-events:none; z-index:200; } .office-sync-ripple--active::before { content:''; position:absolute; width:100px; height:100px; margin:-50px; border-radius:50%; border:1.5px solid rgba(139,92,246,0.55); animation:sync-ripple 0.85s ease-out forwards; } @keyframes sync-ripple{from{opacity:0.7;transform:scale(0.1)}to{opacity:0;transform:scale(8)}}. Respect prefers-reduced-motion. WHY: Data freshness is the product's core trust mechanic. A 0.85-second ripple makes the invisible visible — users know the floor just pinged reality and came back with an answer. Without it, 30-second polling feels like hoping.

ideas.title: Activity Log Session Brackets — Hire to Complete Is a Story
ideas.description:
The activity log is a flat reverse-chronological feed. When a 'hire' and 'complete' event for the same worker appear together in the visible 20 entries, they are just two separate lines — the duration of that session is invisible. Post-process the displayed entries in ActivityLog: scan for matching worker pairs where types are 'hire' then 'complete' within the window. For matching complete entries, compute sessionMs = new Date(complete.timestamp) - new Date(hire.timestamp) and attach a sessionLabel. Wrap those entries in a
with a duration badge: {formatDuration()} session. CSS: .session-group { border-left:2px solid rgba(74,222,128,0.28); padding-left:10px; margin-left:-12px; position:relative; } .session-duration { font-size:0.58rem; color:#4ade80; opacity:0.7; font-variant-numeric:tabular-nums; margin-left:auto; } .session-group .activity-entry--complete { background:rgba(74,222,128,0.04); }. WHY: Events are facts. Sessions are stories. 'Sofia clocked in / Sofia completed QA review #21' as two disconnected entries tells you what happened. Wrapped in a bracket labeled '45m session' tells you how fast it happened. The activity log is the closest thing this app has to a heartbeat — it should beat meaningfully.

ideas.title: Manager Monitor Reactive Screen — Bloberto's Display Speaks
ideas.description: The mgr-desk__monitor div receives data-vibe but renders as a plain dark rectangle regardless. It's center stage, the largest desk prop in the room — and it's mute. Add vibe-reactive CSS pseudo-element animations: crushing = slow green scanlines (healthy terminal); on-fire = red flicker with static bursts; slow-day = blank screen with a blinking cursor; in-flow = steady blue-white scrolling text lines; after-hours = single white dot pulse (screensaver). All CSS-only, zero JS. CSS additions: .mgr-desk__monitor { position:relative; overflow:hidden; } .mgr-desk__monitor[data-vibe='crushing']::before { content:''; position:absolute; inset:0; background:repeating-linear-gradient(180deg,rgba(74,222,128,0.09) 0px,transparent 2px,transparent 5px); animation:monitor-scan 1.8s linear infinite; } @keyframes monitor-scan{from{background-position:0 0}to{background-position:0 20px}}. .mgr-desk__monitor[data-vibe='on-fire']::before { content:''; position:absolute; inset:0; background:rgba(239,68,68,0.12); animation:monitor-flicker 0.15s step-start infinite; } @keyframes monitor-flicker{0%,70%{opacity:1}71%,100%{opacity:0.6}}. .mgr-desk__monitor[data-vibe='slow-day']::after { content:'_'; position:absolute; bottom:3px; left:5px; color:rgba(196,181,253,0.45); font-size:7px; font-family:monospace; animation:blink 1.1s step-start infinite; }. Add @media(prefers-reduced-motion:reduce) { .mgr-desk__monitor::before,.mgr-desk__monitor::after { animation:none; } }. WHY: Bloberto's monitor is the most prominent prop on the stage. Every employee faces it. Giving it one CSS animation per vibe state costs zero runtime and zero JS — but transforms the desk from decoration to narrator.

ideas.title: Ghost Desk OOO Nameplate Badge — Make the Absence Self-Documenting
ideas.description: Ghost workers appear at desks as faded characters with OOO bubbles on hover — a wonderful feature that most viewers don't decode on first look. The desk nameplate shows the worker's name but gives no static visual cue that this desk is actually unoccupied. Fix by adding .desk--ghost styling to the nameplate: .desk--ghost .desk__nameplate { color:rgba(196,181,253,0.45); letter-spacing:0.04em; } .desk--ghost .desk__nameplate::after { content:' · OOO'; font-size:0.5em; color:var(--text-muted); font-weight:400; } .desk--ghost { border-color:rgba(139,92,246,0.2); border-style:dashed; } .desk--ghost .desk__monitor { opacity:0.3; filter:grayscale(0.5); }. In Office.jsx the desk already receives the ghost state (occ.ghost) via the deskMap — add className={`desk ${hasError?'desk--error':''} ${isWorking?'desk--working':''} ${occ?.ghost?'desk--ghost':''}`} to the desk div. Also render OOO as a small pill below the monitor on ghost desks: CSS .desk__ooo-label { font-size:0.38rem; background:rgba(139,92,246,0.15); border:1px solid rgba(139,92,246,0.3); border-radius:3px; color:rgba(196,181,253,0.6); padding:1px 4px; margin-top:2px; display:block; text-align:center; }. WHY: The ghost roster system is one of the most thoughtful features in this product — it shows who belongs here even when they're away. But it's an invisible easter egg right now. A dashed border and a four-character badge makes it immediately readable to anyone who glances at the floor.

ideas.title: Keyboard Shortcut Cheat Sheet — The ? Key That Unlocks the Product
ideas.description: The app has meaningful keyboard shortcuts (1, 2, R) that are entirely invisible — no hint, no legend, no affordance. New users have no idea they exist. Add a discoverable shortcut sheet triggered by pressing ?. In App.jsx add const [shortcutSheetOpen, setShortcutSheetOpen] = useState(false). In the existing keyboard shortcut useEffect add: else if (e.key === '?') setShortcutSheetOpen(v => !v). Render a fixed overlay panel when open: <div className={'shortcut-sheet' + (shortcutSheetOpen ? ' shortcut-sheet--open' : '')} role='dialog' aria-label='Keyboard shortcuts'>. Contents: a simple two-column list of <kbd> + description pairs: 1 Switch to Office, 2 Switch to Dashboard, R Refresh data, ? Show shortcuts, Esc Close. Also add a small ? button in the header near the sync button: <button className='shortcut-hint-btn' onClick={() => setShortcutSheetOpen(v => !v)} title='Keyboard shortcuts (?)'  aria-label='Show keyboard shortcuts'>?</button>. CSS: .shortcut-sheet { position:fixed; bottom:24px; right:24px; background:rgba(13,10,35,0.92); backdrop-filter:blur(10px); border:1px solid var(--border); border-radius:12px; padding:16px 20px; z-index:900; min-width:220px; } .shortcut-sheet--open { animation:shortcut-in 0.25s cubic-bezier(0.34,1.56,0.64,1) both; } @keyframes shortcut-in{from{opacity:0;transform:translateY(8px)}to{opacity:1;transform:none}}. WHY: Every keyboard shortcut that goes undiscovered is a UX debt. The ? cheat sheet is the universal contract of power-user software — it signals 'this product has depth and respects your time.' Twenty lines of code that make the whole app feel more intentional.

ideas.title: Office Window Time-of-Day Label — The Sky Tells You When You Are
ideas.description: The office window already renders a time-aware sky gradient (daylight/golden hour/dusk/stars) — a genuinely beautiful feature. But the sky is silent: there's no ambient label telling viewers what time of day the office is in. The timeLabel variable is computed (sunrise/daylight/golden hour/dusk/midnight) but only used in aria-label. Surface it visually. Add a {timeLabel} rendered inside .office-window__sky. CSS: .window-time-label { position:absolute; bottom:5px; left:0; right:0; text-align:center; font-size:0.42rem; color:rgba(255,255,255,0.4); letter-spacing:0.12em; text-transform:uppercase; font-weight:600; pointer-events:none; user-select:none; }. Also add a second contextual sub-label when isDaytime is false: if hour >= 22 || hour < 5 add class .office-window--deep-night and add a CSS animation to stars: .office-window--deep-night .office-window__star { animation:star-twinkle 2.5s ease-in-out infinite; } @keyframes star-twinkle{0%,100%{opacity:0.3;transform:scale(1)}50%{opacity:1;transform:scale(1.3)}} with --star-delay CSS variable for stagger. WHY: The window already does the hard work of telling time through color. Adding the word 'golden hour' in tiny caps at the bottom frame transforms a pretty gradient into a complete ambient clock — the kind of detail that makes users stop and say 'wait, is that the actual time?'

ideas.title: Live PR Link Preview in Worker Card — The Dashboard Becomes a Command Center
ideas.description: Add optional pr_url field to the workers.json schema. When a worker's task string contains a GitHub PR URL pattern (github.com/owner/repo/pull/N) or when the worker object has a pr_url field, parse owner/repo/prNumber from it. In WorkerCard, detect the URL: const prMatch = worker.task?.match(/github\.com\/([^/]+)\/([^/]+)\/pull\/(\d+)/) || (worker.pr_url && worker.pr_url.match(...)). When a match exists, fire a one-time fetch (no auth, public repos only) to https://api.github.com/repos/{owner}/{repo}/pulls/{number} and cache result in a module-level Map. Render below the task description: ⎇{prData.title}@{prData.user.login}. CSS: .worker-pr-preview { display:flex; gap:6px; align-items:center; background:rgba(255,255,255,0.03); border:1px solid var(--border); border-radius:6px; padding:4px 8px; font-size:0.6rem; color:var(--text-muted); text-decoration:none; margin-top:6px; } .worker-pr-preview:hover { border-color:rgba(139,92,246,0.5); color:var(--accent); } .worker-pr-title { flex:1; overflow:hidden; text-overflow:ellipsis; white-space:nowrap; }. Fail silently (no UI if fetch fails or rate-limited). WHY: This is the idea that turns a novelty dashboard into a tool a real engineering team keeps open all day. When you can click from a worker's name to their open PR, the dashboard isn't a monitor anymore — it's the connective tissue of how the team sees itself working. That's a different product category entirely.
